refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-003 Refined: The NGHE solution shall enable operators and tele-operators to achieve a measured energy efficiency of energy_per_ton ≤ E_target (customer-defined threshold) over the agreed evaluation period, through integrated monitoring, control optimization, and feedback mechanisms.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR is outcome-focused but lacks clarity on the mechanisms by which efficiency is achieved. Refinement introduces the key actors (operators, tele-operators), the measurement basis (energy_per_ton metric), and the temporal scope (evaluation period). This makes the requirement verifiable and traceable to system capabilities.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure total energy consumed (kWh) and total tonnage moved during the evaluation period; compute energy_per_ton; compare against E_target. Acceptance: measured value ≤ E_target with documented worksite conditions and operational policies applied.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall continuously monitor energy consumption (kW) and work output (tons moved) in real-time.
CR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall provide operators and tele-operators with energy efficiency feedback and recommendations during operation.
CR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall optimize vehicle control parameters (e.g., engine throttle, hydraulic flow, transmission mode) to minimize energy per unit work output.
CR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall log and report energy efficiency metrics (energy_per_ton) for post-evaluation analysis and compliance verification.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a centralized energy management system that monitors real-time power draw, calculates efficiency metrics, and sends optimization commands to vehicle subsystems via a closed-loop control loop.

DP-B: Distribute energy monitoring and optimization logic across vehicle control modules (engine, hydraulics, transmission) with local optimization and periodic synchronization of efficiency data to a central dashboard.

DP-C: Implement a hybrid approach: vehicle-resident energy monitoring and local optimization (DP-B logic) combined with a tele-operator-accessible energy efficiency dashboard and advisory system that provides real-time feedback and manual override capability.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Hybrid energy management with vehicle-resident monitoring/optimization and tele-operator-accessible advisory dashboard. Vehicle subsystems (engine, hydraulics, transmission) perform local energy-aware control; telemetry streams energy metrics (kW, tons moved, energy_per_ton) to a central advisory system accessible to both on-site and remote operators. Tele-operators receive real-time efficiency feedback and can issue optimization recommendations or manual overrides via Tasking interface.

potential_sources: Energy monitoring logic is distributed across vehicle subsystems (engine, hydraulics, transmission) but coupled through a common telemetry protocol—acceptable because each subsystem has a clear local optimization objective.
Tele-operator advisory system depends on timely, accurate telemetry from Vehicle—acceptable because Telemetry interface is a known, established interface.

Manual override capability (tele-operator to vehicle) couples advisory system to vehicle control—acceptable because Tasking interface is a known, established interface and override authority is clearly defined in operational policies.
coupling_check.notes: DP-C avoids tight coupling by leveraging existing interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking) and maintaining clear separation of concerns: vehicle subsystems optimize locally; advisory system informs and recommends; operator retains decision authority. No hidden dependencies or circular logic detected.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Vehicle subsystems (engine, hydraulics, transmission) have sufficient sensor coverage and control authority to perform local energy optimization without central coordination.
ASSUMPTION: Telemetry interface can stream energy metrics at sufficient frequency (≥1 Hz) and accuracy (±5% energy, ±2% tonnage) to support real-time advisory.
ASSUMPTION: Tele-operator training and worksite operational policies clearly define when and how manual overrides are permitted, ensuring safety and compliance.
RISK: If vehicle subsystems lack local optimization capability, DP-C degrades to a monitoring-only system; efficiency gains depend entirely on operator behavior.
RISK: If telemetry latency exceeds 2 seconds, advisory feedback becomes stale and tele-operator decisions may be based on outdated efficiency data.
RISK: Operator override of optimization recommendations could lead to unsafe or non-compliant operation if not governed by clear policies.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Confirm that vehicle subsystems (engine, hydraulics, transmission) have documented local energy optimization algorithms and that Telemetry interface meets latency and accuracy requirements. If not, revise DP-C to include explicit design of vehicle-resident optimization logic and telemetry protocol specification.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution module is the top-level system boundary that integrates Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. For CR-NGHE-003, the NGHE Solution IM encompasses: (1) energy monitoring and telemetry aggregation logic, (2) energy efficiency advisory system (dashboard, recommendations), (3) coordination of vehicle-resident optimization with tele-operator feedback, and (4) logging and reporting of energy_per_ton metrics for compliance verification.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle subsystems stream real-time energy consumption (kW), work output (tons moved), and derived efficiency metrics (energy_per_ton) to NGHE Solution. Frequency: ≥1 Hz. Accuracy: ±5% energy, ±2% tonnage.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution sends optimization commands and manual override directives to Vehicle subsystems (engine throttle, hydraulic flow, transmission mode). Initiated by tele-operator via advisory dashboard or by automated optimization logic.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution receives safety constraints and alerts from Safety Services (e.g., load limits, terrain hazards, operator fatigue). NGHE Solution may issue energy-efficiency-related safety alerts (e.g., 'efficiency degradation detected; recommend maintenance check') to Safety Services.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution communicates with Teleoperation module to deliver real-time efficiency feedback to tele-operators and receive manual override commands. Also supports remote logging and reporting of energy metrics.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural top-level container for energy efficiency KPI management. It integrates monitoring (from Vehicle via Telemetry), advisory logic (internal), and coordination with Teleoperation and Safety Services. Minimal diffusion: only Telemetry and Tasking interfaces cross the boundary; SafetyAlert and Network Link are well-defined integration points.

Vehicle module contains engine, hydraulics, and transmission subsystems capable of local energy optimization. However, energy efficiency KPI management (advisory, reporting, compliance verification) is not a core Vehicle responsibility; these belong at the NGHE Solution level. High diffusion risk if energy advisory logic is embedded in Vehicle—would require Vehicle to understand operator feedback and compliance requirements, violating separation of concerns.

Teleoperation module provides the interface for tele-operators to receive efficiency feedback and issue commands. However, the core energy monitoring, optimization logic, and compliance reporting belong in NGHE Solution, not Teleoperation. Teleoperation is a consumer of efficiency data, not the owner of the KPI.

Safety Services manages safety constraints and alerts. Energy efficiency is a performance KPI, not a safety function. While Safety Services may constrain energy optimization (e.g., 'do not reduce engine power below safe threshold'), the primary responsibility for energy efficiency KPI belongs in NGHE Solution. High diffusion risk if energy KPI logic is embedded in Safety Services.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the appropriate owner of CR-NGHE-003 because it is the system-level integrator responsible for coordinating vehicle capabilities (monitoring, local optimization), operator feedback (via Teleoperation), and safety constraints (via Safety Services) to achieve the energy efficiency KPI. Energy monitoring, advisory logic, and compliance reporting are system-level concerns that transcend individual subsystems.
containment_risks: RISK: If NGHE Solution does not have direct access to real-time telemetry from Vehicle subsystems, energy monitoring will be delayed or incomplete, degrading advisory quality.

RISK: If Vehicle subsystems do not implement local energy optimization algorithms, NGHE Solution can only monitor and advise; actual efficiency gains depend on operator behavior, which may not be sufficient to meet E_target.
RISK: If Teleoperation module does not provide a user-friendly dashboard for efficiency feedback, tele-operators may ignore or misinterpret advisory recommendations, reducing effectiveness.
RISK: If Safety Services constraints are not clearly communicated to NGHE Solution, energy optimization may inadvertently violate safety policies.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that NGHE Solution has documented ownership of energy monitoring, advisory logic, and compliance reporting. Ensure that Vehicle subsystems expose energy metrics via Telemetry interface and accept optimization commands via Tasking interface. Verify that Teleoperation module provides a dashboard for efficiency feedback and that Safety Services communicates constraints to NGHE Solution via SafetyAlert interface.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (hybrid energy management with vehicle-resident optimization and tele-operator advisory) directly addresses the refined FR (achieve energy_per_ton ≤ E_target through integrated monitoring, control optimization, and feedback). The DP specifies how monitoring (Telemetry), optimization (vehicle subsystems + advisory), and feedback (Tasking, dashboard) work together to enable efficiency. No logical gaps or misalignments detected.

diffusion_risks: DIFFUSION RISK (Low): Energy monitoring logic is distributed across vehicle subsystems (engine, hydraulics, transmission) but aggregated at NGHE Solution level. Acceptable because each subsystem has a clear local optimization objective and NGHE Solution provides system-level coordination.

DIFFUSION RISK (Low): Tele-operator advisory system depends on timely telemetry from Vehicle and must coordinate with Safety Services. Acceptable because Telemetry and SafetyAlert are established interfaces with clear semantics.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate IM boundary for CR-NGHE-003. It owns energy monitoring aggregation, advisory logic, and compliance reporting. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services are collaborators, not owners. Interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and minimize coupling.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible: measure energy_per_ton during evaluation period and compare against E_target. NGHE Solution must log energy consumption (kWh) and tonnage moved (tons) with timestamps and operational context (worksite conditions, policies applied). Acceptance criteria are objective and measurable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: NGHE Solution is responsible for enabling the KPI through monitoring, optimization, and feedback. Vehicle subsystems are responsible for local energy-aware control. Teleoperation is responsible for operator interface. Safety Services is responsible for safety constraints. Each module has a defined role.

recommended_revisions: Document the energy monitoring protocol (Telemetry interface): specify data fields (kW, tons moved, energy_per_ton), frequency (≥1 Hz), accuracy (±5% energy, ±2% tonnage), and latency (≤2 seconds).

Document the optimization command protocol (Tasking interface): specify which vehicle subsystems can be controlled (engine throttle, hydraulic flow, transmission mode), command format, and authority rules (automated vs. tele-operator override).
Define the energy advisory dashboard: specify what metrics are displayed, how recommendations are generated, and how tele-operators interact with the system.
Define the compliance reporting mechanism: specify what data is logged, how energy_per_ton is calculated, and how results are reported for acceptance verification.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-003 is not atomic; it comprises four sub-FRs (monitoring, feedback, optimization, reporting) that require further decomposition. Each sub-FR has distinct design concerns and implementation modules. Proceeding to the next decomposition level will clarify the detailed design of energy monitoring, advisory logic, vehicle optimization, and compliance reporting.
next_level_sub_frs: CR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall continuously monitor energy consumption (kW) and work output (tons moved) in real-time.
CR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall provide operators and tele-operators with energy efficiency feedback and recommendations during operation.
CR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall optimize vehicle control parameters (e.g., engine throttle, hydraulic flow, transmission mode) to minimize energy per unit work output.
CR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall log and report energy efficiency metrics (energy_per_ton) for post-evaluation analysis and compliance verification.

assumptions: Vehicle subsystems (engine, hydraulics, transmission) have sufficient sensor coverage and control authority to perform local energy optimization.
Telemetry interface can stream energy metrics at ≥1 Hz frequency and ±5% energy, ±2% tonnage accuracy.
Tele-operator training and worksite operational policies clearly define when and how manual overrides are permitted.
Safety constraints from Safety Services do not fundamentally conflict with energy optimization objectives.
NGHE Solution has access to real-time telemetry from Vehicle and can issue optimization commands via Tasking interface.
open_risks: If vehicle subsystems lack local optimization capability, efficiency gains depend entirely on operator behavior, which may not be sufficient to meet E_target.
If telemetry latency exceeds 2 seconds, advisory feedback becomes stale and tele-operator decisions may be based on outdated data.
Operator override of optimization recommendations could lead to unsafe or non-compliant operation if not governed by clear policies.
Energy efficiency KPI may be difficult to achieve if worksite conditions (terrain, load distribution, weather) are unfavorable; acceptance criteria must account for operational context.
evidence_needed: Documented energy monitoring protocol (Telemetry interface specification).
Documented optimization command protocol (Tasking interface specification).
Design of energy advisory dashboard and recommendation algorithm.
Design of vehicle-resident energy optimization logic (engine, hydraulics, transmission).
Compliance reporting mechanism and energy_per_ton calculation methodology.
Worksite operational policies and safety constraints that govern energy optimization.
Tele-operator training materials and procedures for efficiency feedback and manual override.
